speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. February 13, 1996, survivor's name is Abram Asner. Interviewer is Linda Feldman. City and State is Windsor, Ontario. Country is Canada. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Linda Feldman. That's L-I-N-D-A F-E-L-D-M-A-N. I am interviewing Abram Asner. The date is February 13, 1996. And we are in Windsor, Ontario, Canada. Could you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abram Asner.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-B-R-A-M. Asner, A-S-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has Abram Asner always been your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nacza.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-A-C-Z-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was Nacza in when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day were you born on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I tell the day exactly what-- the way-- the way it is now? May 12, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitzchak Asner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nazca.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, I don't know. I was a little boy when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaya Rochl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nacza.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your parents get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I was born. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the oldest child in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rivka.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Rivka born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rivka must be born in 1906, '07, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Who was the second child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yehudal. Yehuda.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1908, '09, something like that. I'm not sure. I think it's 1908. Yeah, it's 1908.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the third child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yakov.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the fourth child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the fifth child?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any children after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aaron and Kusiel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Aaron born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kusiel?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1924, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had some kind of-- all kind of business. He was buying cattle and other stuff like that. Really, exactly, I was a kid. I don't know exactly what he was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work out of the house, or did he go from town to town selling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't going from town to town selling. He was in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother help in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother just was doing-- taking care of the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father ever away from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he left Poland, I think, in 1908.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To United States. He'd been in United States approximately for six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kansas City.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a junkyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he sell in the junkyard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I wasn't there. Just I saw the place when I come here, and I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in business for himself, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Him with his brother, his brother Morris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had Morris gone to Kansas City before him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Morris went-- went before.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in 1912 or '13. Exactly, I don't know, exactly. I think in 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your father come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea why he come back. I just remember he wanted to go back. He-- he had from all kinds of companies, papers with boats and everything. Then he got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when your father came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't. I wasn't even born at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, of course. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Sit, sit. It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Watch your foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Watch it. Watch it. OK. OK. I'll reattach you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. What do you remember of the house that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we used to sleep-- it was a-- we were six boys and a girl. We used to have two in a room, three in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother, Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hanging in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What furnishings did you have in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bed. And we used to have a little dresser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any pets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Dog, cat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any favorite games?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I used to like soccer, play soccer. I really like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With some non-Jewish boys, because the Jewish wasn't too many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of your friends that you played soccer with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got to think about them. No, I don't remember them. It's a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a good soccer player?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Real-- I was real good. I was fast and strong, and I was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you boys play soccer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close was a-- a big place close to the church, to the Catholic church down there. And other places. We used to play in schoolyards.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times a week did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember that. When we have an occasion. We used to play.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't play on Shabbos.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, real not. Shabbos was Shabbos.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate the Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to celebrate Shabbos, I remember, when my father was alive. We used to go to the shul, Shabbos and all of the holidays. Only when we used to come back, we used to sing, zemiros and all kind of stuff, you know. Still, I remembered the songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to sing one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like [INAUDIBLE] or some other songs, we used to sing with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have a good voice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think he used to sing nice. Maybe it's in your family. I don't know. My brother used to sing pretty good, my younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a baritone or a tenor voice?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, I don't know exactly the voice, what-- my younger brother was very talented in-- musical.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What did you used to eat on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tehillim. [LAUGHS] That's it. That's it, just Tehillim.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the psalm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother prepare for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, like every Jewish mother, cholent, um, [INAUDIBLE], desserts. Like every mother, some other Jewish-- like, food, what is challah, used to bake challah, everything, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to go to shul on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the shul located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul was located a little bit-- outside the town. I was little when I was in-- in Nacza. When my father died, we used to go every day to shul to say Kaddish, my father. I remember we always used to say Kaddish after my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there always a minyan in the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- like in Eishyshok?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Nacza.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nacza, not always. When we been little, then always was a problem with a minyan. When we been older, then it was a problem with a minyan on our kids was bar mitzvah. We had half a minyan in our house. Then they brought the Sefer Torah in our house. And they used to make in our house the minyan. It was easier to make a minyan. And when we left, they say, meaning the ghetto-- they forced us, when the German came in, they forced us to go to the ghetto. We took the Sefer Torah with us. And we used to make a minyan during the-- when we was in the occupation-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day we used to make a minyan, and everything was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived in Nacza?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nacza, about 10 families.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would that be in all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about average about 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a rabbi in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A shochet. We have a shochet, not a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the shochet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shochet was Lazer Feldman. He got [? killed ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You mentioned that your father died. How did your father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember what-- he went like, I think, Purim to shul. And then he come back. He used to have asthma. And then he come back. I don't know if he got sick. And he get pneumonia. And they took him to a hospital in Eishyshok. And he been for-- for a while down there. When he die, Pesach, the seventh day of Pesach was my-- it's yahrzeit. I used to say, always yahrzeit. Still, I'm saying Kaddish now after my father, the seventh day of Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Eishyshok.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't die at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do after your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother has a little store. And naturally, my brothers, they used to work with some-- all in wood, and all kind of-- all kind of stuff to-- from-- from like, from the forest to the station, to load the-- I think it was loading to send it to Germany, the wood. And I mostly was helping out our relatives from the United States. My-- my father's brother, Morris, he used to help us out a lot. And my grandfather, he used to daven in shul, like the holidays, on Passover. And the money, he used to send to my mother, because my mother was a widow with seven kids. And the money used to send for my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you and your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was the oldest child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get enough money from America to live comfortably?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a big help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the children working?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- I was working. The pay was very-- very, very little pay. They don't pay-- mostly nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you do to help out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every penny what I used to make, I give-- I give to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you earn your pennies?

speaker: interviewee
text: For hard work. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing blacksmith's work. When I was-- when I was 12, I started on blacksmith's work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you begin learning to be a blacksmith?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nacza.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was your master?

speaker: interviewee
text: A neighbor was a blacksmith. He would just-- he have a slave labor. That's how you-- the pay was very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim David Schmidt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did you have to get up in the morning to go to a shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I used to get up early and work late. And the pay was very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: How early was early?

speaker: interviewee
text: 7 o'clock. At least 7 o'clock, maybe earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in his shop when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I used to do all kind of work, farming work, and for farmers, and for-- shoe horses, and wagons, and all kind of work. Mostly-- mostly at the times, farming.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years did it take for you to learn your trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Takes a quite while. Takes a few years. Took a few years. Then I was in the army. I was doing the same work, so a blacksmith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still, still I don't get paid. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any schooling? Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very-- I was going to school, to public school, very little school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How old were you when you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was seven when I went to school. And I was going to school, and I was pretty good in school. I wanted to-- to learn. I was good at-- and then when my father died, my mother took us to Eishyshok, me and my younger brother. I was eight, and he was six, I think. And the older brother went around and learned in the [INAUDIBLE]. And we was going to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Who was the teacher in the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a Polish-- Polish lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I remember. Elena Polokuwina.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she would be about 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of teacher was she? Was she strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she was strict, and she was a big anti-Semite. She was strict, and she was big anti-Semite.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know what this anti-Semite means.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do to the Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, she don't do anything. She couldn't do anything. Or she's-- the way she spoke to Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally, she-- she don't like. She don't like Jewish kids. She don't like.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subjects did you learn in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a kid, I took any-- everything. Arithmetic and writing and reading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your school have more than one grade in each class? How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. We got more than one grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there in the class that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a lot. Maybe about 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were in different grades?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in different grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life in the cheder like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned. That's always discipline too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your teacher there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- in Eishyshok was a teacher, Shayel Lidzki.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a middle-aged man. I think he was in the war. He was missing a finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was teaching-- they was teaching reading, writing. I learned Chumash and Tanakh. After I get older, I learn [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eishyshok is how far from Nacza?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was 17 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back and forth every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was staying there. My mother took a room. And I was staying there. And with the board, we used-- eat teg. You what teg means? Teg, they arrange for us, for me and my brother, every day to another family go to eat. Like every Tuesday, to this person, we come to eat. Like at Monday, to this person come to eat. And some-- then when we bartered, people from the what we eat the teg, they used to give us a dollar, a zloty, Polish zloty. It's enough for one day to eat, [? even ?] one we bartered for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of meals did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it depends. Depends on-- some was pretty good, and some was not very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your memory of a very good meal that you got? What did you like getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depend on what-- which person. I have seven persons a week, seven balabustas, you know. [LAUGHS] And then some one was not very good. Just I don't have a choice. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was the best balabusta? Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember one name was [INAUDIBLE]. She was a very pretty girl. And she was-- and she used to give me the food. She was a very, very nice to me when she was giving it, very nice, the best food that you can get.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that include meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Include you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It include meat. Oh, I used to get meat, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the other people who gave you meals? Do you remember the other families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, yes, though not everyone. Some areas. On like Shabbos, they used to make [INAUDIBLE] and all kind of stuff, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you get home while you were going to cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to come home for the holidays and for Pesach. And this way, I used to be all summer there and all winter there, just for the holidays. And then beginning another semester, I used to go back. My mother is arrange again, every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Nacza by on foot, or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by horse and buggy. My mother-- my older brother used to pick us up with horse and buggy. We had a horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would your mother do when you came back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a mother. [LAUGHS] She was happy. She used to come, used to play and-- play with the kids together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would she have your favorite dishes waiting for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, I wasn't any favorite. And I was not particular to the dishes. I was good anything. I know my mother did the best way she could because she had a hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after your schooling was over, you began learning how to be a blacksmith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I work from the beginning, like with that Chaim David Schmidt. And then I went in another town in Eishyshok. I worked down there. Down there I get better paid. And I [? settled it ?]. And then I went-- from then, I went to Radun. From Radun, I used to go every day with a bicycle, from Nacza to Radun, 11 kilometers. And then Friday, he keep me long. And then it comes in Shabbos, Friday night. Then from the [INAUDIBLE] the rabbi, they find out. They tried to stop my bus to keep me so long to not to go in [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, I don't know. They was-- I heard it was stuck, and they did nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with Mr. Schmidt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed for a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the circumstances of your leaving? Why did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: For money. I don't get any paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, I lost my job. And I was for quite a while without work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was home. They used to come to Eishyshok I was staying in Eishyshok. I helped my uncle. And then the army, then I went to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you conscripted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was-- I was drafted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when exactly you were drafted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. May-- February 16, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get your basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what unit were you attached to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was attached to artillery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the regiment number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I tell you in Polish or in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: The First Regiment Artillery-- Light Artillery Legionaries from Joseph [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the commanding officer of the regiment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- what do you mean? The-- the Colonel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the first name. I remember the second name, Lapicki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only Jewish recruit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a few more. Eight, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, yes. There was one [? Glombotski, ?] [? Schnea ?] [? Glombotski ?], and was one [? Ullman ?], from another town. One was [? Rappaport. ?] One was [? Kruvski, ?] a good friend of mine, [? Ravel ?] [? Kruvski ?] from [INAUDIBLE]. He was a very good friend of mine. And there was some others I don't remember anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to be Jewish in the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult. I'll give you a sample. When I was a recruit and I come  in in the army, and my brother-- was a list from the same regiment, in the same unit, exactly. When I-- when I come in, and they brought me in the lines, and that officer was reading the list from their recruits, and he find my name, and he said, that's the same-- that's the brother from the same Asner what was over here? And somebody said, yes. Said, don't answer anything. I'll go by if I'll recognize you. He come. He was walking to me. When he come to me, and he stopped, and he looked at me, he said, you Asner. I said yes. He said, you'll be like your brother, it will be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number one.
